Title: Japan's extra budget to include funding from fresh debt, source says
Article:
TOKYO: Japan's government will likely issue fresh debt as part of funding for a planned extra budget to cushion the economic blow from the Middle East war, a government source with direct knowledge of the deliberation told Reuters on Monday.
Any additional debt issuance would further strain Japan's already worsening finances and may accelerate rises in long-term interest rates.
The yield on the benchmark 10-year Japanese government bond (JGB) rose 10 basis points to 2.8 per cent on Monday, marking the highest level since October 1996.
Japan's government is looking at compiling a supplementary budget to ease household energy costs, as fuel prices rise due to the oil shock triggered by the Iran war, sources told Reuters last week.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kelana Jaya LRT Line disrupted after train fault causes delays
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Light Rail Transit (LRT) service for the Kelana Jaya Line was delayed this morning following a technical fault involving a train.
Rapid KL said the disruption was caused by a train component failure which rendered it unable to move.
As a result, longer waiting times are expected at station platforms, particularly at Ara Damansara and Kelana Jaya stations.
"Passengers are advised to use alternative routes to continue their journeys, such as the Ampang/Sri Petaling Line, Kajang Line and Putrajaya Line," it said in a statement today according to Berita Harian.
Following the disruption, Rapid KL operations activated alternative train services and shuttle buses to assist passengers.
At the same time, Rapid KL technical teams were deployed to the site to carry out repair works to restore services as soon as possible.
Auxiliary police and staff have also been stationed on platforms and concourse areas to assist passengers and ensure smooth movement.
"Regular announcements on train schedules are also being made at stations.
"Passengers are also advised to refer to Rapid KL's social media platforms or the MyRapid PULSE app for the latest updates on the Kelana Jaya Line service," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Large-scale Ukrainian drone barrage kills four in Russia
Article:
KYIV, (Ukraine): A wave of almost 600 Ukrainian drones attacked Russia overnight killing four people, authorities said on Sunday, as Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy called the barrage an "entirely justified" retribution for Moscow's own pummelling of Ukraine.
Air defences shot down 556 drones overnight across the country, Russia's defence ministry said, with another 30 drones intercepted after dawn in one of the largest Ukrainian barrages of the ongoing conflict so far.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said Kyiv's attack on Russia — days after a massive drone and missile bombardment of the Ukrainian capital killed at least 24 people — was "entirely justified."
"Our responses to Russia's prolongation of the war and its attacks on our cities and communities are entirely justified.
This time, Ukrainian long-range sanctions reached the Moscow region, and we are clearly telling the Russians: their state must end its war."
On Saturday night, AFP journalists were granted rare access to an undisclosed location where Ukraine launched its long-range drones from, in what turned out to be one of the largest pummelling of Russia during the conflict.
Battalion members prepared the plane-like drones before they whooshed towards Russia, leaving trails of sparks and flames behind.
The Ukrainian defence ministry said that Moscow and the surrounding region "have experienced the largest-scale attack since the full-scale invasion began" in February 2022.
Ukraine's priority "remains the consistent build-up in the use of long-range strike capabilities to the fullest extent possible against a wide range of military targets," Commander of Ukraine's Unmanned Forces, Robert Brovdi, told AFP in a recent exclusive interview before the strikes.
In Russia's capital region, "a woman was killed as a result of a UAV hitting a private house," governor Andrey Vorobyov posted on Telegram, adding that the early morning attack also claimed the lives of two men.
One of the victims was an Indian citizen working in Russia, the Indian embassy in Moscow said in a statement.
Within the capital, one of the strikes wounded construction workers at a job site near an oil and gas refinery, Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said.
"Refinery production has not been disrupted. Three residential buildings were damaged," he added.
While the capital region is often subjected to drone attacks, the city of Moscow, around 400 kilometres (250 miles) from the Ukrainian border, is less frequently targeted.
"The hit was so powerful that it almost knocked me out (of bed), and I weigh a lot. I opened my window and saw smoke rising," Konstantin, a 39-year-old resident of the area where a high-rise was damaged in the Putilkovo neighbourhood, outside Moscow, told AFP on the site.
In the Belgorod region which borders Ukraine, one man was killed overnight in a drone attack on a lorry, regional authorities said.
The Ukrainian air force said meanwhile it had intercepted 279 Russian drones out of a total of 287 launched.
- Refinery, oil depot hit -
Diplomatic efforts to end the conflict have been at a standstill, with Kyiv unwilling to accept Moscow's maximalist demands for territory in the eastern Donbas region, saying it would be tantamount to surrender.
While the United States has pushed for both sides to come to the negotiating table, the talks have noticeably stalled since Washington's attention turned to the US-Israeli war on Iran in late February.
After the expiration of a US-brokered three-day truce on Tuesday to mark the anniversary of victory over Nazi Germany in World War II — which both sides accused the other of violating — Moscow and Kyiv have returned to trading attacks.
In response to daily bombardments by the Russian military for more than four years, Ukraine has regularly struck within Russia.
Kyiv frequently claims long-range strikes that it says target oil processing facilities in a bid to dent the oil revenues that fund Russia's war chest.
The latest attack on Sunday hit "the Moscow Oil Refinery, the Solnechnogorsk oil depot, and several microelectronics manufacturing facilities for the first time", Ukraine's defence ministry said on social media.
Ukraine's General Staff said that among the targets hit was a plant in the Moscow region "which specialises in the production of high-tech products and microchips for high-precision weapons."
"The war is returning to where it came from," the ministry added.
The war has cost hundreds of thousands of lives and forced millions to flee their homes, making it Europe's deadliest conflict since World War II. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Samsung Electronics and its South Korean union resume pay talks as strike risks loom
Article:
SEOUL: Samsung Electronics and its South Korean labour union are set to join a new round of government-mediated pay talks on Monday, in a bid to avert a strike at the tech giant, which accounts for nearly a quarter of the country's exports.
The talks resume days after a first round of government-mediated negotiations over pay and bonus schemes collapsed, ahead of a strike scheduled to begin on Thursday at the world's largest memory chipmaker.
South Korean government officials, including the prime minister and finance minister, have voiced concerns that a strike should be avoided at all costs, warning it could pose significant risk to economic growth, exports and financial markets.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung said in a social media post on Monday that corporate management rights should be respected as much as labour rights in the country's free-market economy.
"In South Korea, which has adopted a liberal democratic order and capitalist market economy, labour should be respected as much as businesses, and corporate management rights should be respected as much as labour rights," Lee wrote on X.
He said that workers should receive fair compensation for their labour, while shareholders who bear risks and losses through investments also deserve a share of corporate profit.
South Korean Prime Minister Kim Min-seok said on Sunday the government would pursue all options, including emergency arbitration, to prevent a strike.
An emergency arbitration order, which can be invoked by the labour minister if the country deems that a dispute is likely to harm the economy or daily life, immediately prohibits industrial action for 30 days while the National Labor Relations Commission conducts mediation and arbitration.
The union said on Sunday it would not give in to pressure on arbitration and would not agree to a pay deal should the company offer a less favourable proposal.
After the collapse of negotiations last week, executives from Samsung's chip division urged the union to refrain from striking, citing concerns raised by hard-won semiconductor customers such as Nvidia, according to media reports.
The executives said some customers had indicated they might temporarily stop accepting shipments during a strike because they could not guarantee product quality, according to reports, citing a participant at the meeting.
Samsung declined to comment on the matter.
Samsung Electronics' shares were trading up 0.7 per cent in morning trade, compared with benchmark KOSPI's 2.5 per cent fall.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zizie Izette arrives at court for RM2.8 million corruption hearing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Actress and entrepreneur Datin Seri Zizie Izette Abdul Samad arrived at the Sessions Court here today for the hearing of her RM2.8 million corruption case.
Zizie Izette, the wife of the late Datuk Seri Bung Moktar Radin, arrived at 8.15am in a black and white outfit and was accompanied by friends.
In March, the defence closed its case after the 48-year-old businesswoman read her 13-page witness statement, followed by cross-examination and re-examination before judge Rosli Ahmad.
In January, Rosli acquitted and discharged Bung Moktar after deputy public prosecutor Law Chin How informed the court that the prosecution would not proceed with the case following his death.
https://www.nst.com.my/news/nation/2026/01/1357837/bung-moktar-discharged-corruption-charges-widows-representation
Bung Moktar had faced three corruption charges linked to Felcra's approval to invest RM150 million in Public Mutual unit trust funds. The prosecution rejected Zizie Izette's request to withdraw three charges of abetting her late husband.
Zizie Izette is represented by Datuk Seri K. Kumaraendran, M. Athimulan, Ridha Abdah Subri, and Teh See Khoon, while Law appeared alongside co-deputy public prosecutor Mohamad Fadhly Mohd Zamry.
On May 3, 2019, Bung Moktar was charged with two counts of corruptly obtaining cash bribes of RM2.2 million and RM262,500 as an inducement to secure Felcra's approval to invest RM150 million in Public Mutual unit trust funds.
He was alleged to have received the bribes from Public Mutual Bhd investment agent Madhi Abdul Hamid via Zizie Izette and a Public Islamic Treasures Growth Fund account in her name.
The offences were said to have occurred at the Public Bank Taman Melawati branch between 12.30pm and 5pm on June 12, 2015, and at 12.16pm on June 19 the same year.
For the third charge, Bung Moktar was accused of receiving RM337,500 in cash from unit trust consultant Norhaili Ahmad Mokhtar in Zizie Izette's name at the same location, date and time.
The charges were framed under Section 17(a) of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2009, which carries a jail term of up to 20 years and a fine of not less than five times the bribe amount or RM10,000, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
Meanwhile, Zizie Izette faces three charges of abetting her late husband in relation to the same matter, at the same locations, dates, and times.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens slightly higher against US dollar amid cautious market sentiment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened slightly higher against the US dollar on Monday although cautious sentiment persisted amid escalating geopolitical tensions in Middle East.
At 8am, the local unit rose to 3.9495/9605 against the greenback from Friday's close of 3.9515/9580.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said market sentiment remained cautious as the prolonged stalemate between the US and Iran continued to raise concerns over a wider conflict in the region.
"The stalemate between the US and Iran seems likely to be protracted and the recent attack on the United Arab Emirates' nuclear facility suggests that the war would likely be prolonged.
"By extension, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz would also be the case as both countries would use the waterway as their leverage," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
The local note appreciated against the British pound to 5.2572/2718 from 5.2749/2835 at Friday's close, strengthened against the euro to 4.5873/6001 from 4.5948/6024 previously and rose against the Japanese yen to 2.4863/4934 from 2.4927/4968.
The ringgit also traded higher against its regional peers.
It advanced against the Thai baht to 12.0684/1083 from 12.0989/1247, improved against the Singapore dollar to 3.0824/0915 from 3.0871/0927 and edged higher against the Indonesian rupiah to 224.4/225.1 from 224.5/225.0 previously.
The local currency was almost unchanged against the Philippine peso at 6.40/6.42 from 6.40/6.41 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: From luxury to research: Billionaire Gabe Newell repurposes superyacht
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: What was once a lavish superyacht owned by billionaire Gabe Newell has been radically transformed into a cutting-edge support and research vessel, drawing attention for its overhaul.
Now renamed Draak, the vessel has undergone extensive modifications, replacing leisure features such as pools and spas with specialised equipment including decompression chambers and heavy-duty cranes, a user on the Reddit forum Bolehland wrote.
The conversion is aimed at supporting deep-sea exploration and research operations, read the post.
"Following the overhaul, Draak has begun crucial sea trials," read the post.
They are a critical phase to test the vessel's performance and capabilities before it becomes fully operational.
The move reflects a growing trend among ultra-wealthy individuals repurposing luxury assets for scientific and exploratory use.
Luxurylaunches, on its website, said the transformation was practical for a yacht expected to launch boats, recover divers, move equipment and operate continuously alongside another vessel.
"New adaptable cabins offer additional high-end accommodation when needed, underscoring Draak's role in supporting personnel movement, operational flexibility and extended expedition cycles.
"The interior conversion was handled by German outfitter Vedder, while Azure Yacht Design & Naval Architecture worked alongside Oceanco on the vessel's naval architecture," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singapore's April exports up 24.5pct, higher than expected
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose by 24.5 per cent in April from a year earlier, government data showed on Monday, led by exports of electronics such as integrated circuits, disk media products and PCs on robust AI-related demand.
The figure was significantly higher than the median forecast of 10.9 per cent growth in a Reuters poll.
Among key markets, exports to the US, China and South Korea rose, while shipments to Indonesia were lower than a year earlier, Enterprise Singapore said.
Last week, Singapore's acting Minister for Transport Jeffrey Siow said the nation needed to make "bold bets" in new areas such as quantum technologies and space, building on adjacent sectors such as semiconductors, advanced manufacturing and aerospace.
"We don't want to just be in the supply chain. We want to be the part of the supply chain that nobody can afford to take out," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rai wins first major at PGA with back-nine birdie blitz
Article:
NEWTOWN SQUARE, US: Aaron Rai won his first major title on Sunday by capturing the PGA Championship, making birdies on four of the last eight holes to become the first Englishman since 1919 to win the event.
Rai sealed a tense back-nine shootout with a spectacular 68-foot birdie putt on the par-three 17th, firing his career-low major round with a five-under-par 65 to finish 72 holes on nine-under 271 at formidable Aronimink.
"Very surreal," said Rai, who has fought a neck injury this year. "It has been a frustrating season so to be standing here is outside my modest imagination."
He defeated two-time major winner Jon Rahm of Spain and American Alex Smalley by three strokes with American Justin Thomas, Germany's Matti Schmid and Sweden's Ludvig Aberg sharing fourth on 275.
The 31-year-old from Wombourne in central England took home a $3.69 million top prize from a record purse of $20.5 million and the Wanamaker Trophy after a dramatic final day.
Rai became only the second English player to win the PGA after Jim Barnes captured the first two titles in 1916 and 1919.
Never better than 19th in 12 prior major starts, Rai won his third DP World Tour title last November in Abu Dhabi and his only PGA Tour title in 2024 at Greensboro.
- Cautious finale -
Playing cautiously in windy weather on Aronimink's sloped greens, the world's top golfers battled the course and each other in a fight that turned as Rai surged into the lead.
Rai answered a bogey at eight with a 40-foot eagle putt at the par-five ninth, then sank birdie putts from four feet at 11 and seven feet at 13, becoming the week's first to reach seven-under.
Schmid, seeking his first PGA Tour title in his fifth major start and first PGA Championship, sank a birdie putt from just inside nine feet at 13 to pull within one of the lead.
Rai responded by reaching the green in two at the par-five 16th to set up a tap-in birdie.
He then delivered a knockout blow by curling in his monster birdie putt over a ridge at 17 to reach nine-under while Schmid made bogey at 15 to leave Rai ahead by four strokes.
"I definitely wasn't trying to hole that putt," Rai said. "The shadow gave the putt a nice line the last 10 feet so that helped. It was about the speed of the putt. Nice to see it go in."
Rahm, the 2021 US Open and 2023 Masters champion, birdied the ninth to reach five-under but made only a bogey and eight pars on the back side.
"I played really good golf," Rahm said. "Just wish I'd have done better with the speed of the greens. Just couldn't seem to get it to the hole, and that's the reason why I didn't hole any more putts.
"Even so, what Aaron did today, catching him could have been very difficult. I feel like I was still close on that playing 16 until he made that long putt."
World number two Rory McIlroy, who won his sixth major title at last month's Masters, shot 69 to share seventh on 276 with American Xander Schauffele and Australian Cameron Smith.
Unheralded Smalley, in only his fifth major, teed off with a one-stroke lead and nine major winners among 22 players within four shots.
A double bogey at the sixth and bogey at the par-three eighth plunged Smalley back until his eagle-bogey-birdie finish.
American Kurt Kitayama fired a bogey-free, seven-under-par 63 to match the lowest final round in major golf history.
"I'm ecstatic," Kitayama said.
Top-ranked defending champion Scottie Scheffler shot 69 to finish on 278.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zizie Izette's corruption trial among key court cases this week
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The continuation of Datin Seri Zizie Izette Abdul Samad's corruption trial is among the key court proceedings taking place this week.
Her case resumes at the Sessions Court today after she concluded her testimony in March.
Zizie Izette, the wife of the late Datuk Seri Bung Moktar Radin, is accused of abetting her husband in obtaining bribes amounting to RM2.8 million linked to Felcra Bhd's investment in Public Mutual Bhd unit trust products.
She allegedly helped Bung, then Felcra's non-executive chairman, obtain approval from the then finance minister II for the agency to invest RM150 million in the products.
The charges involve
alleged bribes
of RM2.2 million and RM262,500 from Public Mutual investment agent Madhi Abdul Hamid, as well as RM337,500 from Public Mutual unit trust agent Norhaili Ahmad Mokhtar.
According to the charges, the payments were allegedly channelled through investment fund accounts registered under Zizie Izette's name.
The offences were allegedly committed at the Public Bank branch in Taman Melawati on June 12 and June 19, 2015.
All charges were framed under Section 28(1)(c) of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission Act for abetment and criminal conspiracy.
The offence carries a maximum jail term of 20 years and a fine of not less than five times the bribe amount or RM10,000, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
Zizie Izette is represented by counsel Datuk Seri K Kumaraendran. The case will be heard before judge Rosli Ahmad.
In Seremban, the Magistrates' Court is scheduled to sentence a teenager convicted of dangerous driving that caused the deaths of three teenagers in March last year.
On Dec 17, 2025, the accused, now 18, changed his plea to guilty when the charge was reread during case management proceedings.
The court then fixed Feb 25 for the presentation of the facts and a probation report.
The accused was charged with dangerous driving causing the deaths of Muhammad Aidil Ramdan Abdullah, 15, Muhammad Aswari Lotpi, 16, and Aisar Azim Abdullah, 17, on March 6, 2025.
He was charged under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act, which carries a jail term of between five and 10 years, and a fine of between RM20,000 and RM50,000 upon conviction.
Meanwhile, the Kuala Lumpur High Court on Thursday is expected to decide on an originating summons filed by M. Indira Gandhi and 13 others challenging the validity of state laws relating to unilateral conversion.
The plaintiffs filed the suit in March 2023 seeking to nullify unilateral conversions across six states, naming the governments of Johor, Perlis, Melaka, Kedah, Negeri Sembilan, Perak and the Federal Territories as defendants.
They argued that the state enactments contradict Article 12(4) of the Federal Constitution, as interpreted in Indira Gandhi's landmark 2018 case on unilateral conversion.
Article 12(4) states that the religion of a person under 18 shall be decided by his or her parent or guardian.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asia shares slip, oil prices pile pressure on bonds
Article:
SYDNEY: Asia share markets slipped on Monday as fresh drone attacks in the Gulf pushed up oil prices and bond yields, while the AI boom is set to be tested by earnings from Nvidia this week.
A drone strike caused a fire at a nuclear power plant in the United Arab Emirates, while Saudi Arabia reported intercepting three drones, as US President Donald Trump warned that Iran must act "fast" to reach a deal.
Meanwhile, the vital Strait of Hormuz remains closed to all but a trickle of shipping as Tehran tries to formalise its control of the waterway that used to carry 20 per cent of the world's oil trade.
"The closure is draining global oil inventories fast," warned analysts at Capital Economics. "Inventories could reach critical levels by end-June, setting the stage for Brent at US$130-140pb, if not higher."
"If the strait is closed through year-end and oil stays around US$150pb into 2027, that would push inflation to near 10 per cent in the UK and euro zone, send rates back to their recent peaks and lead to global recession."
Brent was trading up 1.2 per cent at US$110.63 a barrel, while US crude climbed 1.0 per cent to US$106.42 a barrel.
G7 finance ministers gather in Paris on Monday to discuss the Strait of Hormuz and critical raw material supplies, even as geopolitical differences threaten to test the group's cohesion.
Concerns energy costs would stay high and thus continue to drive inflation saw global bond markets hammered on Friday.
Yields on US 10-year notes were up at 4.584 per cent, having surged 23 basis points last week, while 30-year bonds stood at 5.109 per cent after jumping 18 basis points on the week.
Investors in turn feared central banks globally would have to tighten to head off an inflationary spiral, and a hike from the Federal Reserve is now seen as a 50-50 chance this year.
Minutes of the Fed's last meeting are out on Wednesday and should show how much pressure there was on the committee for a shift to a neutral stance, and away from an easing bias.
Japan's Nikkei 225 eased 0.4 per cent, having fallen 2 per cent last week though that was from record highs. South Korean stocks fell 2.1 per cent, as the red-hot market cooled just a little after demand for semiconductors drove it to all-time peaks.
MSCI Asia Pacific ex Japan Index lost 0.6 per cent. China's markets hit their highest in more than four years last week, but will have to weather data on April retail sales and industrial output later in the session.
**AI, RETAIL EARNINGS TO TEST THE BULL RUN**
S&P 500 futures fell 0.4 per cent and Nasdaq futures lost 0.5 per cent in early trade.
While Wall Street has been supported by upbeat earnings, analysts at Citigroup noted half of the boost to earnings came from one-time items such as tariff add-backs and asset mark-ups. Both the gains in profits and the overall indexes were also tightly based.
"We identify 20 stocks that contributed the majority of index earnings upside," wrote analyst Scott Chronert in a note. "Forward guidance increases also show a similar narrow focus."
"Broadening is a necessary condition for meaningful index upside from here," he added. "This will require a better line of sight to the Iran conflict wind-down."
The all-important AI trade will be tested by earnings from Nvidia due on Wednesday, where expectations are sky high for the world's most valuable company.
Nvidia shares are up 36 per cent since the March low, while the PHLX Semiconductor Sector Index has surged more than 60 per cent, amid voracious demand for chips as tech companies spend massively to build AI-related infrastructure.
Also due this week are results from a host of retailers led by Walmart, which will provide an insight into how consumers are faring with high energy prices.
In forex markets, risk aversion has tended to benefit the greenback as the world's most liquid currency. The US is also a net energy exporter, giving it a relative advantage over Europe and much of Asia.
The euro sat at US$1.1620, after losing 1.4 per cent last week. The pound wallowed at US$1.3318, having dived 2.3 per cent last week as political instability added to already intense pressure on the gilt market.
The dollar held firm on the yen at 158.64, with only the threat of Japanese intervention preventing another speculative assault on the 160.00 chart barrier.
In commodity markets, gold was flat at US$4,540 an ounce, having drawn little support so far as a safe haven or as a hedge against inflation risks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL leads KL traffic crackdown, issues 211 summonses in tourist hotspots
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A multi-agency enforcement operation led by Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) issued 211 traffic summonses and towed 10 vehicles during a crackdown in Jalan Sultan, Jalan Pudu and Bukit Bintang.
The traffic operation in some of the city's busiest and most tourist-heavy areas was carried out in collaboration with the police and the Road Transport Department (RTD) on Friday.
DBKL, in a social media post, shared photographs of the operation showing narrow streets clogged by vehicles disrupting traffic flow, while tow trucks removed offending vehicles and summonses were issued to tourist buses believed to have been illegally parked.
During the operation, DBKL issued 55 compound notices and towed 10 vehicles for traffic obstruction offences, including parking on public roads and in prohibited areas, under the Road Traffic Rules 1959 and the Road Transport Act 1987.
Police issued 21 compound notices, while RTD issued 135 notices against motorists for various traffic offences, obstruction, failure to obey traffic signals and other offences under their respective laws and regulations.
The towed vehicles were taken to the DBKL Vehicle Tow Storage Depot for temporary storage and documentation.
"The joint operation will continue at other traffic hotspot locations," DBKL said.
"Road users are reminded to comply with traffic rules and signals set by the authorities," it added.
Netizens hailed the tough enforcement action. A user going by the name Jason Smith Cheah said: "Good job for Petaling Street and Pudu area. Keep it up."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil touches two-week high after drone attack on UAE nuclear power plant
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices extended gains on Monday as efforts to end the US-Israeli war on Iran appeared to have stalled, after a nuclear power plant in the United Arab Emirates came under attack and as US President Donald Trump is expected to discuss military options on Iran.
Brent crude futures climbed US$1.44, or 1.32 per cent, to US$110.70 a barrel by 2337 GMT after touching the highest since May 5 earlier in the session.
US West Texas Intermediate was at US$107.26 a barrel, up US$1.84, or 1.75 per cent, following a rise to its highest level since May 4.
Both contracts gained more than seven per cent last week as hopes of a peace deal that would end ship attacks and seizures around the Strait of Hormuz dimmed.
Last week's talks between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping ended without an indication from the world's top oil importer that it would help resolve the conflict.
Drone attacks on the UAE and Saudi Arabia and rhetoric from the US and Iran raised concerns of an escalation in the conflict.
Emirati officials said they were investigating the source of the strike on the Barakah Nuclear Power Plant and that the UAE had the full right to respond to such "terrorist attacks."
Saudi Arabia, which intercepted three drones that entered from Iraqi airspace, warned it would take the necessary operational measures to respond to any attempt to violate its sovereignty and security.
"These drone strikes are a pointed warning - renewed US or Israeli strikes on Iran could trigger more proxy attacks on Gulf energy and critical infrastructure by Iran or its regional proxies," Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG, said.
Trump is expected to meet top national security advisers on Tuesday to discuss options for military action regarding Iran, Axios reported.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PN open to electoral pacts ahead of next general election
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Perikatan Nasional (PN) is open to exploring electoral cooperation with any party or group sharing its principles and vision ahead of the next general election.
Its secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan said the coalition is also open to welcoming strategic partners without requiring them to become component parties.
He said the matter was decided during a PN supreme council meeting chaired by Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar yesterday.
Following the decision, Takiyuddin said PN will establish a special committee comprising representatives from its component parties to refine the necessary terms and conditions.
"This approach is believed to better suit the country's current political landscape and support constructive political cooperation for national stability and public welfare, where people are increasingly pressured without convincing solutions.
"It is also seen as opening space for all parties to actively engage in the nation's political process more meaningfully and healthily through an official, mature, and orderly national political cooperation," he said in a statement last night.
Meanwhile, Takiyuddin said the same meeting decided that the previous decision to accept MIC as a PN component party is no longer relevant and is nullified.
He said PN found that MIC has yet to finalise its entry as a component party, a decision made on Dec 9, 2025, despite multiple requests to do so.
"Given the lack of confirmation and MIC's official statement that it never made a formal application to join PN, it is hereby determined that the previous decision to accept MIC as a component party is unfortunately no longer relevant and is void," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: G7 finance chiefs seek to tackle imbalances as trade strains unity
Article:
PARIS: G7 finance ministers gathering in Paris on Monday will try to find common ground on tackling global economic tensions and coordinating critical raw material supplies, even as geopolitical differences threaten to test the group's cohesion.
The two-day meeting follows a summit between US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing that yielded few concrete economic breakthroughs, as tensions over Taiwan and trade simmered beneath a display of diplomatic cordiality.
At the core of the Paris agenda will be what French Finance Minister Roland Lescure described as deep-seated global economic imbalances that are fuelling trade friction and risk a turbulent unwinding in financial markets.
"The way the global economy has been developing for the past 10 years or so is clearly unsustainable," he said, pointing to a pattern in which China under-consumes, the United States over-consumes and Europe under-invests.
UPDATE FROM US-CHINA SUMMIT
Lescure, who will host the talks, said the G7 offered an opportunity for frank dialogue among allies at a time of widening disagreements with Washington.
"These discussions are not easy. I'm not going to tell you that we agree on everything, including, of course, first and foremost with our American friends," he told journalists ahead of the meeting.
Finance ministers will be looking for an update on US-China relations following the Trump-Xi summit and the latest US efforts to re-open the Strait of Hormuz, as the Trump administration allowed a sanctions waiver on Russian seaborne oil to lapse on Saturday.
Merely agreeing each side bears some responsibility for the trade and capital flow imbalances would be a success, French officials involved in preparations said, though the US side is likely to be reluctant.
FALLOUT FROM MIDEAST CONFLICT
"I'd be shocked if they're going to sign on to the idea this is the US's fault in some way," said Philip Luck, director of the economics programme at Center for Strategic and International Studies.
Ministers are also due to discuss the economic fallout from the Mideast conflict and volatility on global bond markets, which are of particular concern to Japan.
Britain's finance ministry said Rachel Reeves would "press for coordinated action to limit inflation and supply chain pressures, and restore freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz" at the meeting, and also reassert the government's desire to reduce trade barriers between Britain and the European Union.
Divisions within the G7 complicate efforts to project unity as ministers prepare for a June 15-17 leaders summit in the spa town of Evian.
CRITICAL MINERAL DEPENDENCE
A second priority will be critical minerals and rare earths, where G7 governments are trying to coordinate efforts to reduce reliance on China, which dominates supply chains vital for technologies such as electric vehicles, renewable energy and defence systems.
Lescure said the G7 would push for stronger coordination to monitor markets, anticipate disruptions and develop alternative supplies, including through joint projects spanning allied economies. The aim is to ensure that "no country can ever again have a monopoly" over such materials, he said.
G7 countries are trying to agree on a common toolbox of measures to stabilise markets and encourage domestic investment, possibly through price floors for producers, pooled purchases and also tariffs.
Nonetheless, the initiative is a long-term project that would yield little at the finance ministers' meeting, said Luck, who worked on the issue in the Biden administration.
"We are in the very early innings of figuring this out," he said. "I don't think there's agreement on a strategy even within the US government, let alone being able to articulate that in a convincing way to our partners in order to get them to sign on."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man wanted over stabbing of woman in car in Kedah arrested
Article:
ALOR STAR: A 32-year-old man wanted for stabbing a woman inside a Mazda 3 in front of a Kepala Batas restaurant has been arrested.
Kota Setar police chief Assistant Commissioner Syed Basri Syed Ali confirmed the arrest and said the suspect would be remanded today.
Last night, it was reported that a civil servant, 35, was injured after she was
stabbed in the neck
by a man known to her.
The victim was taken to Hospital Sultanah Bahiyah and is in stable condition.
The case is being investigated under Section 326 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing grievous hurt with a dangerous weapon.
The offence carries a penalty of up to 20 years in prison, caning, and a fine.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Southeast Asia suited for smaller narrowbody jets, AirBorneo could benefit: Experts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Smaller narrowbody aircraft such as the Airbus A220 and Embraer E2 family could open new opportunities for airlines in Southeast Asia, particularly on thinner and underserved routes.
Endau Analytics aviation analyst Shukor Yusof said Southeast Asia's geography made the region suitable for smaller jet aircraft.
"Southeast Asia has a very good opportunity for regional jets. By geography, our region is perfect for something that's less than a 100-seater," he told Business Times when contacted last week.
Shukor was commenting on AirAsia X Bhd's landmark order for 150 Airbus A220-300 aircraft in a deal valued at about US$19 billion at list prices, marking the largest single firm order ever for the A220 programme.
The agreement, announced earlier this month in Canada, also includes options for up to 150 additional aircraft.
An aviation industry expert said smaller narrowbody aircraft could be useful for airlines looking to match aircraft capacity more closely with thinner routes.
"It's right-sizing for thin routes. It's a strategy of frequency over capacity and it's good for secondary city connectivity," the expert, who asked not to be named, said.
The industry expert added that smaller narrowbody aircraft could also serve as network feeders and offer lower trip costs and better fuel efficiency.
He said the aircraft could work in markets requiring capacity larger than turboprop aircraft such as the ATR but smaller than an Airbus A320 or Boeing 737.
"It may work for the Asean region where the market is a bit too small for an ATR but too big for an A320 or B737," he said.
Shukor said smaller narrowbody aircraft could potentially benefit Sarawak-owned AirBorneo, particularly for operations within Borneo.
"Borneo is a fantastic place for regional jets. It's a big island but small enough for hopping around small airports. You don't need big airports."
"It would make perfect sense if AirBorneo considered regional jets. The cost associated with it is also lower and the size of the population also means that you don't necessarily need 150- or 160-seater aircraft," he added.
However, Shukor and the industry expert said airlines would still to carefully balance operating economics, fleet strategy and technical requirements before introducing a new aircraft type.
Shukor said larger narrowbody aircraft such as the A320 and B737 often have an advantage because the operating cost difference with smaller single-aisle aircraft was not significant in many cases.
"The cost of running that compared to a regional jet is not that much. Airlines tend to go for bigger ones (aircraft)," he said.
The industry expert said smaller narrowbody aircraft worked best when integrated into a broader airline network rather than used mainly for standalone point-to-point operations.
"Regional jets make perfect sense for dual-brand or multi-platform strategies because they fit into a bigger network," he said.
He cited the example of Singapore Airlines and its budget carrier subsidiary Scoot where the latter's operations with smaller aircraft worked because they fed into Singapore Airlines' network.
"Success in Asean depends on using them specifically to unlock virgin markets where narrowbody aircraft are simply too much capacity," he added.
Shukor said Scoot is an example of how smaller aircraft could be deployed in Southeast Asia.
Scoot currently operates Embraer E190-E2 aircraft on selected regional routes including flights between Singapore and Melaka.
"The Embraers have worked very well for Scoot because the number is just nice for them and it suits their current business model," he said.
Both Shukor and the industry expert said introducing smaller narrowbody aircraft would require airlines to make additional investments in training, maintenance and operational support.
Shukor said the A220 differed significantly from the A320 family.
"It's important to understand that even though the A220 is an Airbus aircraft, it's very different from the A320 and all the other Airbus aircraft.
"The cockpit is slightly different. Pilots would need separate type certification and training even though they are already flying the A320 or A330," he said.
The industry expert also said Southeast Asia's maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) ecosystem is still developing in terms of supporting smaller narrowbody aircraft.
"Currently, the technical and MRO ecosystem in the Asean  region is not really fully geared up yet for regional jets. But if more airlines onboard regional jets, maybe in time the ecosystem would be able to support them," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI scams raise pressure on banks, says Visa
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The rapid rise of artificial intelligence (AI)-driven scams is putting growing pressure on banks and payment firms to upgrade their systems and strengthen fraud detection as digital transactions continue to expand across the region.
Visa head of value-added services for regional Southeast Asia Varun Mahindru said trust has become one of the most valuable assets in the digital economy as fraudsters increasingly use AI tools to create more convincing scams.
"Generative tools can write convincing scams in seconds, clone voices from short recordings, and spin up fake websites that mirror legitimate ones," he said in a recent Visa Insights Circle media rountable.
Citing Sumsub's Identity Fraud Report published in November 2025, Mahindru said deepfake-enabled scams in Malaysia surged 408 per cent year-on-year, reflecting the growing sophistication of fraud attempts in the region.
He said the challenge for financial institutions is no longer just the scale of fraud, but the speed at which new threats emerge and evolve.
"The battleground is not just how much fraud occurs, but how quickly it evolves, and how fast defenders can adapt without eroding the trust they aim to protect," he said.
Mahindru noted that AI is also being deployed by payment firms to strengthen fraud prevention.
He said AI models now assess transactions in real time by analysing anomalies such as unfamiliar devices, unusual locations, and behavioural shifts.
According to him, financial institutions are increasingly combining traditional rule-based systems with machine learning models that continuously retrain themselves to improve fraud detection while minimising friction for legitimate users.
He added that Visa's recent acquisition of Featurespace has enabled the company to strengthen its end-to-end fraud monitoring capabilities across cards, account-to-account transfers, and real-time payments.
Mahindru said collaboration across the payments ecosystem is becoming increasingly important, particularly as scams spread rapidly across markets.
"When a wave of fake investment sites or phishing texts appears in one market, data and insights are quickly passed to partners elsewhere," he said.
He added that Visa has introduced its Visa Scam Disruption practice, which combines network-level data, AI and human expertise to detect and disrupt scam activity.
The focus on fraud prevention and trust comes as banks globally continue accelerating digital transformation efforts to modernise legacy systems and improve customer experience.
Meanwhile, Pismo head of business development for Asia Pacific Varun Dudeja said many banks remain reliant on legacy systems built decades ago, making it difficult to keep pace with customer expectations and technological change.
"A lot of times, banks are discussing how to move out of legacy systems, but they are unclear about how to modernise and what to modernise first," he said during his presentation at the event.
He said banks often struggle to maintain system stability during migration exercises, which can result in service disruptions and application downtime.
Dudeja added that legacy systems also limit banks' ability to fully utilise customer data because information is often stored in silos without real-time feedback capabilities.
He said banks are increasingly looking to reduce operational costs, improve efficiency and shorten product development cycles.
According to him, Pismo's cloud-native platform allows banks to modernise gradually by replacing smaller modules instead of overhauling entire systems at once.
Dudeja said the company currently supports 280 million application programming interface users globally and conducts more than 600 production deployments monthly while maintaining uninterrupted system availability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia to gain more ground in medical tourism
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's medical tourism industry is expected to grow stronger in 2026 as rising healthcare costs and global uncertainty push more international patients to seek affordable and trusted treatment options in Southeast Asia.
The country has emerged as one of Asean's leading medical tourism hubs, competing closely with regional players such as Thailand and Singapore.
While Thailand remains the region's largest medical tourism destination due to its established wellness and hospitality ecosystem, Singapore continues to dominate the premium healthcare segment with highly specialised treatments.
But Malaysia has built a strong reputation for comparatively lower treatment costs, internationally accredited hospitals, English-speaking healthcare professionals and Muslim-friendly services.
The country's healthcare travel revenue jumped 23.2 per cent to RM3.35 billion in 2025 from RM2.72 billion in 2024, while the number of medical tourists increased by about 15.6 per cent to 1.85 million patients from 1.6 million previously.
The industry also expanded significantly beyond pre-pandemic levels, with healthcare travel revenue nearly doubling from RM1.7 billion in 2019, while patient arrivals grew by about 51.6 per cent from 1.22 million previously.
Industry observers said Malaysia's appeal has strengthened amid ongoing geopolitical tensions, particularly the United States-Iran conflict, which has disrupted travel to some Middle Eastern healthcare hubs and prompted more patients to consider alternative destinations in Asia.
BMI pharmaceuticals and healthcare analyst Ben Yau said Malaysia's affordability, quality healthcare and cultural familiarity place it in a strong position to attract patients from the Middle East, South Asia and Asean markets.
"The US-Iran conflict is disrupting travel to Middle Eastern medical hubs like Dubai, pushing more patients from the region to consider alternatives such as Malaysia," he told Business Times.
STABLE AND TRUSTED DESTINATION
Malaysia Healthcare Travel Council (MHTC) said the country continues to be viewed as a stable, neutral and trusted healthcare destination despite broader geopolitical uncertainty affecting global travel patterns.
"Patients increasingly value destinations that offer both medical excellence and a sense of safety," MHTC said, adding that Malaysia benefits from strong public-private coordination and relatively seamless accessibility for international patients.
Apart from the Middle East, Malaysia continues to attract medical tourists from India, while Indonesia remains the country's largest source market.
However, Yau cautioned that economic pressures in Indonesia, including a weaker rupiah and rising travel costs, could affect inbound patient numbers if consumers reduce overseas healthcare spending.
At the same time, he noted Malaysia could still benefit as patients seek lower-cost alternatives to destinations such as Singapore.
MHTC said patient behaviour is evolving rather than weakening, with travellers placing greater emphasis on convenience, predictability and support throughout the healthcare journey.
"Patients are not only comparing clinical outcomes anymore. They are looking at how easy it is to travel, how predictable the process is and how supported they feel throughout," the council said.
It added that Malaysia's strong regional connectivity, manageable visa requirements and high English proficiency among healthcare professionals continue to support its attractiveness among foreign patients.
Coordinated facilitation services, including airport meet-and-greet arrangements, also help improve the overall patient experience.
RISING COSTS REMAIN A CHALLENGE
Yau said increasing costs related to energy, logistics and imported medical equipment are beginning to place pressure on Malaysia's private healthcare industry.
He added that rising prices for medicines and medical devices could eventually affect Malaysia's pricing competitiveness if operating costs continue to increase.
Still, Malaysia's diversified network of medical device suppliers from Singapore, China, Germany, the US and Japan should help reduce direct supply chain risks linked to geopolitical disruptions.
BMI and MHTC expect the Malaysia Year of Medical Tourism 2026 initiative to support stronger sector growth through expanded international partnerships, digital healthcare initiatives and stronger promotion efforts in key markets.
The council said the strategy moving into 2026 will focus not only on patient volumes, but also on attracting higher-value treatments, encouraging longer patient stays and strengthening the overall healthcare journey from pre-arrival planning to post-treatment follow-up.
"Malaysia's ability to integrate healthcare with hospitality, supported by coordinated services such as
our members' hospitals, patient facilitation and ground support, eventually strengthens Malaysia Healthcare positioning in the medical tourism industry," MHTC said.
However, Yau warned that intensifying competition from Thailand, Vietnam and the Philippines, alongside rising operational costs, could test Malaysia's long-term cost competitiveness if healthcare providers eventually pass higher expenses on to patients.
"Thailand is ramping up its focus on medical tourism as a strategic priority, leveraging its well-established hospitality sector and wellness offerings to attract international patients.
"Meanwhile, Vietnam and the Philippines are gradually building their capacity and expanding their healthcare offerings, which could start to draw more patients from within Asean and potentially impacting Malaysia's regional market share," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's economic reset: Restoring income, reviving demand
Article:
Malaysia must urgently reset its economic direction by confronting two pressures increasingly weighing upon ordinary households-the relentless rise in the cost of living and the steady erosion of purchasing power. Many Malaysians feel trapped in an economic reality where wages struggle to keep pace with escalating prices of food, transportation and essential services.
The consequence is a gradual weakening of domestic demand, consumer confidence and economic dynamism itself. Unless disposable income is meaningfully restored and household spending is restored, the nation will drift into a slow-consumption economy where growth figures may appear encouraging on paper, yet prosperity remains increasingly detached from the lived experience of the rakyat. Increasing disposable income is therefore the true engine of sustainable economic growth.
The first battle is to reduce the cost of essentials which include, food housing, transport healthcare and utilities. Reducing pressure in these areas would immediately improve disposable income
Reducing pressure on housing, healthcare and utilities requires coordinated structural reform aimed at lowering systemic costs rather than short term price controls. In housing, expanding affordable supply through efficient approvals and better urban planning; in healthcare, strengthening public systems, improving preventive healthcare and enhancing procurement efficiency can reduce out-of-pocket burdens
It is important that increases in food prices should be treated as a national issue, not just an issue affecting farmers and the agricultural community. When prices food escalates, a large portion of household income would have to be allocated to daily necessities, leaving little disposable income for other forms of consumption.
To address these problems, Malaysia should pursue a Food Cost Stabilisation Strategy aimed at modernising the agricultural sector through mechanised farming, precision agriculture, cooperatives and smart irrigation systems. At the same time, it needs to reduce supply chain inefficiencies through direct-farm-to-market systems, digital wholesale platforms and reduce dependence on multiple intermediaries.
Equally important is the strengthening of cold-chain infrastructure. A substantial amount of vegetables, fruits, poultry, and other perishables are lost during storage and transportation due to inadequate refrigeration and logistics systems. Reducing such wastage lowers effective supply shortages and helps stabilise prices without necessarily requiring large increases in production.
Malaysia must strive to pursue strategic food resilience. This does not mean complete self sufficiency in all food products, which may not be economical, but a reduction in excessive dependence on staples such as rice, vegetables and poultry
Ultimately, if food prices become more stable and affordable, households immediately regain spending power. That additional disposable income flows back into the economy through higher consumption, stronger business activity, increased private investment, and a wider multiplier effect across multiple sectors of the economy.
Salaries cannot rise sustainably unless productivity rises alongside them. In any economy, wage growth is ultimately tied to the value created by each worker; when productivity remains low, higher wages tend to feed inflation rather than real income gains. For this reason, Malaysia must shift from labour-cost competitiveness to productivity competitiveness, where growth is driven by efficiency, technology adoption, and higher value-added activities.
This requires several key initiatives, including SME digitalisation, as many SMEs have yet to fully adopt digital systems. Introducing digital accounting, inventory management, e-commerce tools, and AI-assisted workflows can significantly improve efficiency. Alongside this, skills transformation is essential, supported by stronger technical and vocational education and industry-linked training, so that workers are better equipped for higher-value roles. With these reforms in place, productivity can rise, leading to better wages, lower costs, and greater overall competitiveness.
With higher disposable income, households spend more and businesses see stronger demand, firm expand operations, which creates a multiplier effects. People consume more confidently when there are not financially anxious. Economic confidence of the household is crucial.
Malaysia should also move towards Agro-technology, semiconductor ecosystem expansion and digital economy, as these sectors represent Malaysia's high productivity value-driven sectors that deliver the largest multiplier effect.
Agro-technology links food security, employment, logistics and manufacturing and in the semi-conductor industry it should focus not just on assembly, but on design testing and packaging deepening its integration into the global value chains. At the same time, AI infrastructure, cloud services, cyber security and regional digital services position Malaysia as the fastest growing segments of global innovation.
Similarly green industries such as solar, battery systems, sustainable infrastructure and energy efficiency technologies open new pathways for long term industrial growth while supporting environmental transition goals. Collectively these sectors create skilled jobs, supplier ecosystems higher wages and export strength.
Malaysia's core challenge is not the lack of growth, but its weak translation into household prosperity. Rising living costs and stagnant purchasing power have eroded consumer confidence and weakened domestic demand.
A true economic reset must therefore focus on restoring disposable income by stabilising essential costs and improving productivity. When households regain financial confidence, consumption, business expansion, and investment will reinforce a more resilient and inclusive growth cycle.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
The writer holds an MBA from the University of Strathclyde in the UK, awarded through the prestigious Chevening Scholarship. With extensive experience in financial markets and a strong background in management education, he has also served in a prominent think tank. The opinions expressed are his own and do not necessarily represent the views of Business Times.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cypark: From pioneer to sustainable energy powerhouse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Once a scrappy renewable energy start-up that learned through hard-won experience, Cypark Resources Bhd has emerged as a disciplined, EPCC force with a RM3 billion order book to make Malaysia's circular economy a reality.
When Datuk Ami Moris took the reins at Cypark, the company was in the thick of a stabilisation phase - restructuring RM1.3 billion in debt facilities with MBSB, tightening governance and rebuilding execution discipline.
Three years on, the board chair turned group managing director is steering a markedly different enterprise: one with a growing EPCC orderbook, Malaysia's only operational waste-to-energy plant, a suite of AI-powered project tools, and a RM3 billion project book to deliver.
In an email interview with Business Times, Ami laid out how Cypark has evolved from Malaysia's pioneer in renewable energy deployment into a leading sustainable energy and circular economy expert and why the company's best years are still ahead.
School of Hard Knocks
Cypark's story is not one of overnight success.
The company cut its teeth on some of Malaysia's earliest large-scale solar deployments, managing complex environmental and engineering challenges that few others were willing to tackle.
Along the way, the lessons were expensive but invaluable.
"Cypark went through the school of hard knocks early on and learned tremendously from our experiences.
"We are now seasoned and armed with experienced, Malaysian talent, to drive our Cypark forward," she remarked.
That hard-won expertise is now paying dividends.
The past two years of financial restructuring and governance reform had given Cypark the platform to convert experience into execution.
The RM1.3 billion refinancing with MBSB is complete, recurring cash flow visibility has been restored, and the company has institutionalised governance structures to give investors confidence in how capital is deployed.
The transition from recovery into growth is most visible in Cypark's EPCC segment.
A landmark example is the company's involvement in the 595 MWac floating solar photovoltaic plant with Battery Energy Storage System at Kenyir Lake, Terengganu - a significant project awarded by the consortium of Terengganu Incorporated Sdn Bhd and TNB Power Generation Sdn Bhd.
The project is notable not just for its scale but for its integrated nature, pairing large-scale floating solar with energy storage to deliver firm, dispatchable green power.
"What makes this project significant is not just its scale, but its integrated nature.
"The inclusion of energy storage alongside large-scale floating solar reflects the industry's shift towards more complex, hybrid energy solutions," Ami said.
Technology, AI And EPCC Advantage
If the early years taught Cypark how to build, the current era is teaching it how to build smarter.
Ami is candid about the role that artificial intelligence and digital tools now play across the company's operations - from project monitoring and cost management to supply chain transparency.
"Technology, AI and renewable energy are at our advantage right now. As we exercise our strength as an EPCC, AI is providing a tremendous advantage to our work.
"We are witnessing unprecedented transparency in our capability to view current projects and share necessary project information with our suppliers and partners," Ami said.
The timing is opportune. Malaysia's energy transition, coupled with surging demand from data centres and industrial users, is driving a robust pipeline of renewable and hybrid infrastructure projects.
With three new projects in hand, Cypark is positioned to demonstrate what an AI-enabled EPCC operation looks like at scale.
"With three new projects in our house, we will be steadfast at developing AI tools to our unique advantage," she said.
The EPCC model is central to Cypark's growth strategy because it is capital-efficient.
Rather than relying solely on asset ownership, EPCC allows faster project turnaround, earlier earnings visibility, and improved cashflow velocity - while still capturing meaningful margins through execution capability.
Closing The Loop: Waste-to-Energy and the Circular Economy
Beyond EPCC, Cypark's waste management credentials set it apart from the renewable energy pack.
Malaysia generates over 38,000 tonnes of municipal solid waste daily, a figure that continues to climb with urbanisation and economic activity.
The government has committed to developing 18 waste-to-energy plants nationwide by 2040, and Cypark is already operating the country's first — the Smart WtE facility in Ladang Tanah Merah, Negri Sembilan, processing 1,000 tonnes of municipal waste per day.
On March 6 this year, Cypark reached an agreement with the government to develop Phase 2 of the plant, further scaling this platform.
The expansion is not merely about capacity; it is a continuation of a long-term waste infrastructure programme that supports national objectives of reducing landfill dependency.
"We have curated a team at Cypark with the purpose and intent to be the driver of renewable energy transition and circular economy projects - with an intent to drive the cost to produce electricity as low as possible and to ensure we eliminate landfills and their negative impact on our environment," Ami said.
The company is also expanding into biogas, biomass, and bio-LNG opportunities that align with Malaysia's agricultural base.
The strategy, she explained, is not about choosing between EPCC and waste management, but about building a balanced platform where EPCC drives near-term growth and execution-led earnings while environmental infrastructure provides long-term visibility and strategic differentiation.
A More Structured Earnings Profile
For investors, the key question is how Cypark's multi-pronged strategy translates into financial performance.
The answer lies in the deliberate layering of income streams.
EPCC provides near-term earnings uplift - lumpy in nature, but meaningful in scale - as projects such as Kenyir move into execution.
Meanwhile, the circular waste and WtE segment provides stable, concession-based recurring income with long-term visibility.
The Phase 2 WtE expansion is expected to deliver a step-up in revenue contribution upon completion and commencement of operations.
Recurring cash flow from existing assets provides a stability base, while EPCC-driven deployments deliver margin uplift and growth acceleration.
As projects are commissioned, leverage ratios are expected to strengthen progressively.
Growth and deleveraging, Ami emphasised, will move in parallel.
"As we do this, we are focused on building a company with a clear and investable profile, combining growth, earnings visibility and disciplined execution, while supporting Malaysia's energy transition through renewable energy infrastructures and advancing waste management and circular economy objectives through WtE and integrated resource recovery solutions.
"Taken together, this positions Cypark as an integrated platform - combining execution capability with recurring infrastructure income - which we believe offers a more balanced and differentiated proposition to investors."
Empowering The Next Generation
Underpinning all of this is a leadership philosophy that Ami describes with characteristic directness.
Her transition from executive chair to group managing director was itself a signal - a move to bring operational propulsion closer to the decision-making centre, while the appointment of Tan Sri Abdul Wahid Omar as an independent chairman reinforces governance oversight.
But the real investment, she said, is in people.
Cypark is strengthening engineering capabilities, project management discipline, digital monitoring systems and sustainability reporting standards.
The ambition is to build Malaysian clean energy engineering capability that can scale regionally.
"At Cypark, we are empowering a new generation of leaders to do the right things, right. A business strategy that will always outpace," she said.
It is a statement that captures the essence of Cypark's evolution.
From the school of hard knocks to a company now positioned at the intersection of Malaysia's energy transition, circular economy ambitions and AI-driven infrastructure delivery, Cypark's trajectory suggests a business that has found its stride.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hydropolitics, mega-dams and the future of strategic power
Article:
For much of modern history, water was treated primarily as a developmental issue. It was associated with irrigation, sanitation, flood mitigation and public utilities. Today, however, that assumption is becoming increasingly outdated. Water is no longer merely an environmental or engineering concern. It is rapidly emerging as a strategic variable shaping economics, geopolitics, national resilience and international stability.
The growing importance of hydropolitics reflects a deeper transformation in the global system. In the past, strategic competition centred largely around territory, military power and energy resources. In the emerging era, power increasingly flows through control over systems, networks and critical flows — supply chains, semiconductors, maritime chokepoints, digital infrastructure and increasingly, water itself.
This is why hydropolitics deserves to be understood not as a niche academic discipline, but as an essential component of future studies and strategic planning.
Recent developments have reinforced this reality. The World Meteorological Organization has repeatedly warned that the world is experiencing unprecedented climatic stress. The period between 2015 and 2025 has been recorded as the warmest decade in modern history. Extreme heatwaves, severe droughts, catastrophic floods and increasingly erratic rainfall patterns are no longer isolated anomalies. They are becoming structural features of the international environment.
At the same time, the intensification of El Niño and La Niña cycles has amplified volatility across Asia and beyond. El Niño typically brings drought conditions and water stress to large parts of Southeast Asia, while La Niña often increases the frequency and severity of flooding. The challenge for governments is therefore no longer confined to managing water scarcity alone. Increasingly, states must simultaneously manage both scarcity and excess.
The strategic implications are profound.
For Malaysia — where trade exceeds 130 per cent of GDP and where climate disruptions increasingly affect food systems, flood management, industrial corridors and logistics infrastructure — water resilience is becoming an economic issue as much as an environmental one.
Water insecurity now affects food systems, energy generation, industrial production, public health, urban resilience and political stability. In this context, hydropolitics becomes inseparable from national security itself.
Mega-Dams, River Power and Strategic Leverage
Perhaps nowhere is this transformation more visible than in the construction of mega-dams.
Across the world, large-scale hydroelectric projects are no longer viewed purely as developmental infrastructure. They increasingly serve as instruments of statecraft, strategic leverage and geopolitical influence. Upstream control over rivers can create downstream vulnerabilities, especially in regions where populations, agriculture and industry depend heavily on shared water systems.
The Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam on the Nile illustrates this reality clearly. For Ethiopia, the dam represents sovereignty, electrification and economic transformation. For Egypt, however, concerns over water flow and long-term river management have elevated the issue into one of national security. The dispute demonstrates how infrastructure intended for development can simultaneously generate geopolitical anxiety.
A similar dynamic is unfolding in Asia. China's large-scale hydropower development along the upper Mekong and the proposed mega-dam projects on the Yarlung Tsangpo in Tibet have intensified regional discussions regarding downstream ecological impacts, water dependence and strategic asymmetry. Countries located downstream increasingly recognise that hydrological geography can translate into geopolitical leverage.
The Mekong basin offers perhaps the clearest example of hydropolitics emerging in Southeast Asia. The issue extends far beyond electricity generation. River flows influence fisheries, sediment movement, agriculture, transportation and food security for millions of people across multiple countries. Water infrastructure decisions taken upstream inevitably produce political, economic and environmental consequences downstream.
In many respects, rivers are becoming strategic corridors not unlike maritime chokepoints. Just as states monitor shipping lanes, ports and energy routes, they must now increasingly monitor water flows, reservoir capacity and hydrological risk.
Malaysia's Water Future in a More Volatile World
For Malaysia, these developments carry important long-term implications.
Malaysia is often perceived as water abundant. Yet abundance does not automatically guarantee security. Climate volatility, rapid urbanisation, industrial expansion and changing rainfall patterns are beginning to expose structural vulnerabilities within the country's water management systems.
Recent years have demonstrated this clearly. Flood events in the Klang Valley and several states have imposed substantial economic and social costs. Water disruptions affecting households and industrial zones have highlighted infrastructure constraints. At the same time, growing demand from manufacturing, energy production, urban centres and emerging sectors such as data centres will place increasing pressure on long-term water sustainability.
The 2021–2022 floods alone caused billions of ringgit in economic losses and displaced tens of thousands of households, underscoring how climate-linked water disruptions now carry direct macroeconomic consequences.
The challenge is therefore not simply one of supply. It is one of resilience.
This requires Malaysia to rethink water governance beyond the traditional confines of utilities and drainage management. Water policy must increasingly be integrated into broader national planning frameworks involving economic resilience, climate adaptation, infrastructure strategy and even geopolitical assessment.
This includes strengthening integrated river basin management, expanding climateresilient urban drainage systems, improving inter-state coordination over water resources, and embedding hydrological risk assessments into infrastructure and industrial planning.
In practical terms, this also means improving climate-adjusted water forecasting, modernising flood mitigation systems, protecting river ecosystems and strengthening coordination between federal and state authorities. Future economic competitiveness will increasingly depend on the ability to secure reliable and sustainable water systems for industry, agriculture and urban growth.
Equally important is the regional dimension. Southeast Asia's interconnected river systems and shared climate vulnerabilities suggest that hydropolitics will increasingly require diplomatic engagement and regional cooperation. Water security cannot be treated solely as a domestic issue when climatic and hydrological risks transcend borders.
Ultimately, hydropolitics is not merely about rivers, dams or rainfall. It is about how states prepare for an era of heightened systemic uncertainty.
In the twentieth century, geopolitical competition revolved heavily around oil, sea lanes and industrial capacity. In the twenty-first century, strategic power will increasingly depend on the management of interconnected flows — energy, technology, finance, logistics and water.
In the emerging geopolitical landscape, the struggle for strategic advantage may increasingly depend not only on who controls trade routes and technology — but also on who can secure water, manage volatility and sustain resilience.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WHO keeps evaluation of hantavirus as 'low risk'
Article:
GENEVA: The World Health Organisation said Sunday it was maintaining its assessment of the hantavirus outbreak as "low risk" as the cruise ship where the outbreak originated approached the Netherlands.
"The public health risk has been reassessed with the most current information available, and the global risk remains low," said a WHO statement.
"While additional cases may still occur among passengers and crew members exposed before containment measures were implemented, the risk of onward transmission is expected to be reduced following disembarkation and the implementation of control measures," it added.
The MV Hondius is expected to dock in the Dutch port of Rotterdam between 10:00 am (0800 GMT) and midday on Monday, according to officials, before disembarking the 27 remaining people on board: 25 crew and two medical staff.
The ship, operated by Dutch company Oceanwide Expeditions, made headlines after three passengers died from hantavirus – a rare virus for which no vaccines nor specific treatments exist.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: War in Middle East: Latest developments
Article:
PARIS: Here are the latest developments in the Middle East war:
- Trump makes fresh threats -
President Donald Trump issued a new warning to Iran on Sunday, saying the Islamic republic had to move quickly to end the conflict that has shaken the Middle East and sent energy prices climbing.
"For Iran, the Clock is Ticking, and they better get moving, fast, or there won't be anything left of them," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform. "Time is of the Essence!"
The war began Feb 28, when US and Israeli forces launched major strikes on the Islamic republic, but peace negotiations have stalled since the April 8 ceasefire and sporadic attacks continue.
- Israeli strikes in Lebanon -
An Israeli missile strike on an apartment in eastern Lebanon Sunday killed an Islamic Jihad commander and his 17-year-old daughter, Lebanese state media said.
Emergency workers were searching the rubble for survivors of the blast on the outskirts of the city of Baalbek.
Meanwhile, Israeli strikes on towns in southern Lebanon killed five people, including two children, and left at least 15 people wounded, the Lebanese health ministry said.
Israeli attacks since the start of the war have killed more than 2,900 people in Lebanon, including more than 400 since the truce began on April 17, according to Lebanese authorities.
- Saudi Arabia intercepts drones -
Saudi Arabia said Sunday it had intercepted and destroyed three drones that had entered from Iraqi airspace.
Defence ministry spokesman Turki al-Maliki said the drones had entered on Sunday morning and that the ministry "reserves the right to respond at the appropriate time and place."
- Israeli kills 5 in Gaza -
Israeli strikes and gunfire on Sunday killed five people in Gaza, where at least 871 people have been killed in persistent violence since an October ceasefire, health officials in the Palestinian territory said.
Prime Minister Netanyahu said Israel is has almost achieved its goal of eliminating all those responsible for orchestrating the Oct 7 attack.
"I promised that every single architect of the massacre and the hostage-taking would be eliminated down to the last one, and we are very close to completing this mission," Netanyahu said during the weekly cabinet meeting.
- No US concessions -
Iranian media said Washington had failed to make any concrete concessions in its latest response to Iran's proposed agenda for negotiations to end the war.
The Mehr news agency said: "The United States, offering no tangible concessions, wants to obtain concessions that it failed to obtain during the war, which will lead to an impasse in the negotiations."
- Drone strike outside UAE nuclear plant -
A drone strike triggered a fire near a nuclear power station in the emirate of Abu Dhabi, authorities said, reporting no injuries or impact on radiation levels.
The fire was "in an electrical generator outside the inner perimeter of the Barakah Nuclear Power Plant in the Al Dhafra Region, caused by a drone strike," the Abu Dhabi Media Office said.
Saudi Arabia's foreign ministry condemned the attack as a "threat" to the region, as did UN nuclear agency chief Rafael Grossi, who posted on X that "military activity that threatens nuclear safety is unacceptable."
- Pakistani mediators in Tehran -
Pakistan's Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi met Sunday in Tehran with Iran's chief negotiator and speaker of parliament, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf.
"Some governments in the region believed that the presence of the United States would bring them security, but recent events showed that this presence is not only incapable of providing security, but also creates the grounds for insecurity," Ghalibaf said after the meeting.
Islamabad has been mediating peace talks between Iran and the US and last month hosted a high stakes meeting between delegations from both sides. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Spanish PM faces historic loss in key Andalusia vote
Article:
SEVILLE: Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez's Socialists appeared set for an unprecedented drubbing in Andalusian regional elections that could be a dress rehearsal for next year's national vote, partial results showed.
